excerpt: The authority of the speaker determines the weight of speech; comprehension requires trust in the communicant.
The disbeliever's inability to deny the Qur'an's eloquence, even while rejecting its divine origin, attests to the text's miraculous nature.
The Qur'an's meaning is inseparable from its Arabic idiom and the prophetic voice that delivered it; untranslatability demonstrates inimitability.
The narrative of Tufayl ibn ʿUmar al-Dawsī illustrates the immediate emotional and spiritual impact of Qur'anic recitation upon the listener.
Tufayl's exclamation conveys the inexhaustible depth of the Qur'an; the miraculous nature resides in the piercing reality of the utterance itself.
The opening of Sūrat Fuṣṣilat declares the Qur'an a revelation from Allah, shifting the debate from human rhetoric to divine truth.
ʿUtbi's transformation illustrates that the Qur'an's power lies not in refutation but in the overwhelming presence of divine truth.
The transience of worldly accumulation reflects the Qur'anic theme of mortality; remembrance of death is a central spiritual discipline.
The principle that Allah alone is worthy of worship is restated as lived orientation, examining actual devotions.
The attributes of Allah—self-sufficient, beyond need, yet infinitely near—are classical theological themes in Islamic thought.
The Qur'anic assurance that Allah is present wherever turned to grounds the believer's journey in divine presence.
The attributes of negation—freedom from need, hunger, sleep, form—distinguish the divine from the created.
Divine omniscience is presented not as threat but as comfort; the knower of all secrets is also merciful.
The description of Allah's creative act across six days and establishment upon the Throne draws from Qur'anic verses.
Provision of milk from the mother's breast despite its emergence from filth and blood exemplifies divine providence.
Allah's sustenance of all creatures, including the vile, renders unlawful means unnecessary for the believer.
The governance of the universe testifies to an ordering Intelligence; this cosmological argument is classical in Islamic philosophy.
La ilāha illā-Allah is a covenant binding the believer in radical submission to divine will.
Karbalā and the martyrdom of Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī exemplify sacrificial devotion transcending worldly attachments in Shīʿī spirituality.
The path of the Messenger is illuminated; all deviation is darkness—this binary is fundamental to the Qur'an's moral geography.
Moulding oneself to the Prophet's example is the means of securing spiritual guidance against the wind of passion.
Divine election traces a genealogical hierarchy toward the transmission of prophecy and establishment of faith.
Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā emphasizes the uniqueness of the Prophetic station and the reciprocal love between Allah and His Messenger.
source_location_hint: divine swearing by forenoon and night

excerpt: Only the speech of the Prophets stands protected from error, for they speak by divine revelation. No ordinary human can claim immunity from mistake.
source_location_hint: On the distinction between prophetic and human utterance

excerpt: My tongue is fallible—willfully, persistently, repeatedly.
source_location_hint: On human limitations and divine wisdom

excerpt: Whether you gaze to the far East or the remote West, Allah is sovereign...Allah is sovereign everywhere.
source_location_hint: On divine omniscience and the attributes of Allah

excerpt: Has He ever said He grew weary? Did He ever claim to slumber? Has Allah ever complained that the work has grown too great...His treasures run low?
source_location_hint: On the attributes of God and divine transcendence

excerpt: Yet you have fashioned your idols...You appear to worship me alone, yet in truth you worship these. Your heart bows to them; your forehead bows to me.
source_location_hint: On sincerity and the purification of intention

excerpt: حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَهُۥ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْ
source_location_hint: Surah al-Fath (48:24) or similar thematic verse on mirage

excerpt: The houses of this world are like the wings of a mosquito in the sight of God
إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكَ فَٱخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ ... فَتَرْدَىٰ
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَمَلَٓئِكَتَهُۥ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّۚ
يَٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا۟ صَلُّوا۟ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا
جَعَلُوٓا۟ أَصْبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ آذَانِهِمْ وَٱسْتَغْشَوْا۟ ثِيَابَهُمْ وَأَصَرُّوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ ٱسْتِكْبَارًۭا
أَخْرَجَ مِنْهَا مَآءَهَا وَمَرْعَىٰهَا۔فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا حَبًّۭا وَعِنَبًۭا وَقَضْبًۭا وَزَيْتُونًۭا وَنَخْلًۭا وَحَدَآئِقَ غُلْبًۭا وَفَكِهَةًۭ وَأَبًّۭا
قُلْ هَذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِى ۖ وَسُبْحَنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ
When we invoke Allah alone, all ninety-nine attributes are comprehended within that single Name.
source_location_hint: General Islamic teaching on divine names and attributes

excerpt: The Earth rotates at approximately one thousand kilometers per hour, a speed that produces the rhythm upon which all life depends.
source_work: Contemporary astronomical knowledge integrated with classical Islamic commentary
source_location_hint: Cosmological observations cited in sermons and tafsīr literature

excerpt: We rain down water in abundance, split the earth with channels, caused grain to grow in it, and vineyards, vegetation, olives, palms, gardens of dense trees, fruit, and pasture—provision for you and your cattle.
Even the weak Muslim recognizes the Creator; the profound deception touches lordship itself (al-Rabb), for one may acknowledge Allah as God yet live as though other lords govern.
source_work: Qadi al-Nuʿman, Daʿaʾim al-Islam; various theological commentaries on monotheism
source_location_hint: Doctrine of tawhīd and belief in divine lordship

excerpt: And when your Lord drew forth from the children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants, and made them testify concerning themselves, 'Am I not your Lord?' They said, 'Yes, we testify!'
source_location_hint: Surah al-Aʿraf, verse 172—the covenant of spirits

excerpt: O son of Adam! I created the seven heavens and seven earths, and all between them, yet creating them has not fatigued Me.
source_location_hint: Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim, Sunan al-Tirmidhī—divine promises regarding sustenance

excerpt: My mercy descends upon you continuously; your misdeeds ascend toward Me continuously. Yet when night falls, I grant you sleep as though you had not disobeyed Me all that day.
source_location_hint: Divine address on mercy and forgiveness

excerpt: Indeed, those who say 'Our Lord is Allah' and then remain steadfast—angels descend upon them saying, 'Do not fear and do not grieve, and rejoice in the Paradise that was promised to you.'
Allah has explained in twelve manifestations: your first and your last, your human and your jinn, your living and your dead.
source_work: hadith qudsī tradition; Islamic jurisprudential commentary
source_location_hint: Doctrinal discussions of divine lordship over all creation

excerpt: Say: The corrupt and the pure are never equal, though the abundance of corruption captivates you.
The example of Ibn al-Khārash: al-Ḥajjāj tested him regarding his sons' whereabouts; Ibn al-Khārash spoke truth, and al-Ḥajjāj granted the sons freedom for his honesty.
source_location_hint: Moral exemplars in Islamic history and jurisprudence

excerpt: Say: O Allah, Possessor of Sovereignty, You grant sovereignty to whom You will, and You withdraw sovereignty from whom You will. You exalt whom You will, and You humble whom You will. In Your hand is all good. Surely You have power over all things.

You merge the night into the day, and You merge the day into the night. And You bring forth the living from the dead, and You bring forth the dead from the living. And You provide sustenance to whom You will without measure.
God brought you forth from your mothers' wombs knowing nothing.
What you have been given of knowledge is very little.
With such meager knowledge in our possession, how can we presume to move forward relying upon it alone? Certainty of error awaits us.
When affliction befalls him, he falls into despair and despondency, complaining to Allah and losing all hope.
Man simply does not possess the capacity to lay the foundations of life upon intellect and experience alone.
I did not teach you poetry, nor is it befitting for you. This is naught but a Remembrance and a clear Qur'an.
I have sent down to you the Book and Wisdom, and I have taught you that which you knew not. Great is the favor of Allah upon you.
Wahb ibn Munabbih declared that the knowledge of all other prophets stands to the knowledge of Muhammad as a single drop to the vast sea.
Thus We have revealed to you a Spirit from Our command. You knew not what the Book was, nor what faith; but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will.
The authority resides in the one who directs; the instrument is merely the hand that executes.
Exalted be He who carried His servant by night from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque.
Thus the Almighty has woven both letters into the verse to teach us the honor shown to the Prophet.
The Prophet's household lacked even fire in the hearth for two consecutive months, yet he showed such compassion to his daughter Fāṭimah.

The Almighty said: I have gazed upon all creation, and from it I selected the Arabs. From the Arabs, I chose the Mudrites. From the Mudrites, I singled out the Quraysh. From the Quraysh, I chose Banū Hāshim. And from Banū Hāshim, I chose myself.

The Prophet prayed: O Allah, You are the Provider of Muhammad's family. O Allah, diminish our sustenance. So that none might say on the Day of Judgment that his children lived in ease while ours starved.
The greeting came forth: Peace be upon you, O Prophet! Peace be upon you, O My Beloved! The very Lord of all beings offered His salutation.
O People of the Book! There has come to you Our Messenger, clarifying much of what you concealed from the Scripture, and forgiving much. There has come to you from Allah a Light and a clear Book.
O Abū Sufyān! I have brought you not merely the honors of this world, but the honors of the world to come.
I am the master of all the children of Adam on the Day of Resurrection. I shall hold the banner of Allah in my hand. The keys to Paradise shall rest in my grasp.
I leave you upon a radiant path—a path upon which night and day are as one, a path from which only the destroyed shall stray.
Whoever seeks a religion other than Islam, it shall not be accepted of him, and in the Hereafter he shall be among the losers.
To Allah belongs the command before and the command after.
Look anywhere: from east to west, from north to south—Allah reigns. Gaze upward: Allah. Gaze downward: Allah.
How many powerful kings have been cast upon their faces, their names and emblems erased, while Allah declared: I alone am the Ruler.
Pharaoh's determination to slay Moses, yet Allah's will that Moses should live; the system of alternating years of slaughter and mercy demonstrating divine sovereignty
Moses' mother's fear and Allah's promise: the river shall cast him upon the shore, Pharaoh shall take him, yet Allah will return him and make him a Messenger in her lifetime
Abraham's response to Gabriel and the Angel of Water: 'I have no need of you, but yes, I am in need of Allah'—demonstrating total reliance upon the Divine alone
source_location_hint: Commentary on Sūrah al-ʿAnkabūt 29:17 and related passages

excerpt: Umar ibn ʿAbd al-ʿAziz's deathbed counsel to his children: choosing piety over illicit wealth, and Allah's blessing upon the righteous descendants of the pious
source_location_hint: Life of the Fifth Rightly-Guided Caliph

excerpt: Those whose sides leave their beds at night—the description of the night-praying servants honored on the Day of Judgment
